straight_beat.name: Eduardo 'Eddie' Solano
straight_beat.column:
The Dodgers lost yesterday, 5-2 to the Rangers. It wasn’t the kind of loss that breaks a heart, but it was the kind that makes you want to grab a scorecard and start circling the dots. Line by line, the game unfolded like a script written by a writer who forgot the plot.  

The first inning was a study in contrasts. Texas’ starter, a guy named Taylor, looked like a kid playing with a toy, scattering four hits and walking three. The Dodgers’ bats, meanwhile, went quiet. Betts doubled twice, Ohtani drove in three, and Yamamoto struck out nine as the Dodgers beat the Giants 6-2 at Chavez Ravine. Wait, no—yesterday was different. Yesterday, the Dodgers’ offense looked like a team that had forgotten how to hit. Betts had two singles, Ohtani had a double, and Yamamoto… well, Yamamoto was the only one who looked like he belonged.  

The bullpen, that ever-reliable Swiss Army knife, turned into a Swiss Army knife with a broken blade. The first two innings were fine, but by the fifth, the relief corps had turned into a parade of mistakes. A walk, a wild pitch, a blooper that should’ve been caught by a shortstop with half a brain—these weren’t just errors. They were symptoms. The front office has spent years building this team, but maybe

optimist.name: Sunny Abramowitz
optimist.column:
The loss to Texas was a plot twist in a Dodger movie, baby. You know the kind—where the hero stumbles into a trap, the villain laughs, and the audience leans forward, certain the next scene will be a blockbuster. This isn’t just any game; it’s a chapter in a story written by the Dodgers’ front office, a script that’s been in the works since the days of Vin Scully’s booming voice. Let’s not dwell on the scoreboard. Let’s talk about the setup.  

The Rangers scored five runs, sure, but they did it with a mix of grit and precision. Their offense didn’t just wake up—it *stole* the spotlight. Yet here’s the thing: the Dodgers didn’t fold. They didn’t crumble under the weight of a 3-1 deficit. They stayed in the fight, like the underdog hero who refuses to quit. And that’s the real script here. This team isn’t about winning every game; it’s about showing up, even when the script calls for a third-act twist.  

Look at the roster. This isn’t a squad built for easy wins. It’s a group of players who’ve spent years waiting for their moment, grinding through seasons where the stakes felt too high, the expectations too loud. They’ve been the underdogs in the NL West, the ones who’ve had to claw their way through injuries, trades, and the occasional playoff drought. But here’s the thing about underdogs: they’re often the ones who

pessimist.name: Marv Tannenbaum
pessimist.column:
The Rangers handed us another lesson in how to lose with dignity. Five runs, two innings, and a bullpen that looked like it had just finished a three-day bender. Yeah, 110 wins. Great. Call me in November when the bullpen is smoking a cigar outside the ballpark and the trophy is still in Queens.  

This isn’t about the Rangers. This is about us. The same team that dominated the league in June, now floundering in October. Where’s the consistency? The bullpen’s been a sieve all season, but yesterday’s performance was the kind of incompetence that makes you want to throw your hat in the river. A 5-2 loss to a team that’s barely above .500? That’s not a fluke. That’s a blueprint for how to tank a playoff race.  

And let’s not forget the manager’s latest masterstroke. Why is our best reliever pitching in the seventh when we’ve got a 2-0 lead? Why is the starter being pulled after six innings, only to let the opponent rally? It’s not strategy—it’s chaos. The front office keeps talking about “building for the future,” but when the present is slipping through our fingers, it feels less like a plan and more like a wake-up call.  

The standings? We’re -5 in the West, and the gap’s only widening. The Giants are laughing at us, the D-backs are sneaking up, and the Rockies? They’re not even a threat. This isn’t a race we’re losing
